topic: A person discovers their mentor has retired without saying goodbye
raw_output:
[story 1]

The office felt hollow. Dust motes danced in the shafts of late afternoon sun slicing through the blinds, illuminating the empty ergonomic chair where Elias used to sit. Sarah stood by the doorway, clutching the neatly folded retirement package—a thick envelope containing paperwork and a single, impersonal card. "Mr. Thorne?" Her voice sounded thin, barely reaching the quiet expanse of the room. Elias hadn't moved. His hands, usually steady when reviewing complex schematics, were clenched so tightly his knuckles were stark white against his skin. He stared at the closed door, a strange, cold pressure building behind his ribs. He wanted to speak, to demand an explanation, but the words caught in his throat, lodging like shards of glass. He took a slow, shallow breath, the air tasting metallic in his mouth. He felt a sudden, overwhelming urge to bolt, to run from the silence that now seemed too vast, too absolute.

[story 2]

When the email arrived, the subject line was simply "Transition." Mark reread it three times, his gaze snagging on the lack of any customary sign-off, no warm farewell, just a curt notification confirming his mentor, Dr. Alistair, was officially stepping down. Mark felt a sudden, dizzying lurch in his stomach, a sensation like falling from a great height. He paced the length of his study, the polished wood floor echoing his hurried steps. Every sound—the distant hum of city traffic, the soft click of the radiator—seemed amplified, sharp and intrusive. He kept checking his phone, half-expecting a reply, a clarification, anything to anchor himself in the reality that Alistair was actually leaving. But the silence on the screen was deafening. He stopped abruptly, leaning against the bookshelf, his breath catching in short, uneven gasps. His vision swam slightly at the edges, and he had to squeeze his eyes shut, fighting the urge to press his palms against his temples.

[story 3]

"He's gone," Lena whispered, the words barely audible over the whir of the ventilation system. She watched the silhouette of David, standing by the window, his back rigid. David hadn't looked at her. He was simply staring out at the cityscape, his shoulders hunched as if bracing against an unseen force. Lena felt a knot tighten in her chest, constricting her lungs. It wasn't sadness she felt, not exactly; it was a profound sense of dislocation, as if the foundation of her entire professional world had just dissolved. She took a hesitant step forward, her shoe scuffing the rug, and tried to call out his name, but the sound that emerged was a reedy croak. David didn't flinch, but the stillness radiating off him was immense, heavy, suffocating. He turned his head slowly, his eyes vacant, and offered nothing back, just a long, unbroken gaze that seemed to absorb all the light in the room.
